name: Corneja Valley and El Mirón Castle Loop
name_original: Pueblos de la Sierra de Ávila — circular
description: This moderate 35.2-mile road cycling route through the Sierra de Ávila villages offers varied terrain, scenic views, and cultural highlights
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Malpartida De Corneja are characterized by the varied terrain of the Sierra de Gredos mountain range, featuring significant climbs and descents. The region includes high mountain passes and expansive valleys, offering diverse landscapes for road cyclists. Roads often traverse open countryside, providing views of the surrounding natural environment. The area's topography presents opportunities for challenging rides with considerable elevation gain. Best road cycling routes around Malpartida De Corneja The most popular road cycling route is Puerto de Peñanegra y Chia desde San Martin - Parque regional Sierra de Gredos — circular desde Piedrahíta, a 51.2 miles (82.4 km) trail that takes 4 hours 41 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant climbs through the Sierra de Gredos. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Peña Negra and Chía Pass Loop from Piedrahíta, a difficult 40.7 miles (65.5 km) path. This route navigates mountain passes with considerable elevation changes. Local road cyclists also love the Puertos de Peñanegra y Alto del Reventón — circular desde Piedrahíta, a 50.1 miles (80.7 km) trail leading through mountain passes and high points, often completed in about 4 hours 16 minutes. Road cycling around Malpartida De Corneja is defined by mountain passes, valleys, and open countryside. The network offers options for moderate to difficult ability levels. The routes in Malpartida De Corneja are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Malpartida De Corneja's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Malpartida De Corneja?
A: The region is characterized by the varied terrain of the Sierra de Gredos mountain range. You'll encounter significant climbs and descents, high mountain passes, and expansive valleys. Roads often traverse open countryside, providing diverse landscapes and opportunities for challenging rides with considerable elevation gain.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes for beginners in Malpartida De Corneja?
A: While the area is known for its challenging terrain, there are some moderate options. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1315486307/corneja-valley-and-el-miron-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corneja Valley and El Mirón Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 35.2-mile (56.6 km) route with less elevation gain compared to the more difficult mountain pass rides.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes with significant climbs?
A: Malpartida De Corneja is ideal for challenging rides. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063028971/puerto-de-penanegra-y-chia-desde-san-martin-parque-regional-sierra-de-gredos-circular-desde-piedrahita" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Peñanegra y Chia desde San Martin - Parque regional Sierra de Gredos — circular desde Piedrahíta&lt;/a&gt;, a 51.2-mile (82.4 km) trail, feature significant climbs through the Sierra de Gredos. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1315444519/pena-negra-and-chia-pass-loop-from-piedrahita" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Negra and Chía Pass Loop from Piedrahíta&lt;/a&gt;, which navigates mountain passes with considerable elevation changes.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063028971/puerto-de-penanegra-y-chia-desde-san-martin-parque-regional-sierra-de-gredos-circular-desde-piedrahita" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Peñanegra y Chia desde San Martin - Parque regional Sierra de Gredos — circular desde Piedrahíta&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1062415266/puertos-de-penanegra-y-alto-del-reventon-circular-desde-piedrahita" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puertos de Peñanegra y Alto del Reventón — circular desde Piedrahíta&lt;/a&gt;, both offering extensive circular tours through mountain passes.

Q: What kind of scenic viewpoints can I expect along the routes?
A: The region offers expansive views of the Sierra de Gredos. Many routes pass through high mountain passes, providing panoramic vistas of valleys and open countryside. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5216726" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Negra Pass&lt;/a&gt; is a notable highlight for its scenic beauty.

Q: What are some interesting landmarks or historical sites to see while cycling?
A: You can explore historical sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4448740" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Bonilla de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5445968" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Mirón Castle&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1315486307/corneja-valley-and-el-miron-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corneja Valley and El Mirón Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically incorporates a visit to the latter.

Q: How long do road cycling routes typically take in Malpartida De Corneja?
A: Route durations vary significantly based on distance and elevation. For example, the 51.2-mile (82.4 km) &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063028971/puerto-de-penanegra-y-chia-desde-san-martin-parque-regional-sierra-de-gredos-circular-desde-piedrahita" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Peñanegra y Chia desde San Martin - Parque regional Sierra de Gredos — circular desde Piedrahíta&lt;/a&gt; can take around 4 hours 41 minutes, while the 40.7-mile (65.5 km) &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1315444519/pena-negra-and-chia-pass-loop-from-piedrahita" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Negra and Chía Pass Loop from Piedrahíta&lt;/a&gt; typically takes about 3 hours 35 minutes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Malpartida De Corneja?
A: Given the mountainous terrain and potential for significant climbs, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions with milder temperatures. Summers can be hot, especially in the valleys, while winters can bring cold temperatures and potentially snow at higher elevations, making some passes inaccessible.

Q: Are there any quiet road cycling routes away from heavy traffic?
A: Many routes in the Malpartida De Corneja area traverse open countryside and mountain passes, often on quieter roads with less traffic. The region's focus on natural landscapes means you can find peaceful stretches, particularly outside of main towns.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Malpartida De Corneja?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the challenging climbs, the stunning mountain scenery, and the sense of accomplishment from tackling the high passes of the Sierra de Gredos.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through towns or villages where I can find cafes or refreshments?
A: Routes often connect smaller towns and villages within the region. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063024990/piedrahita-puente-del-cong" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piedrahíta, Puente del Cong&lt;/a&gt; route starts and ends in Piedrahíta, a town where you can find amenities. Planning your route to pass through these settlements will allow for refreshment stops.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of the Villages of the Sierra de Ávila — circular route?
faqs.answer: This route is considered moderate for race biking. While the overall tour is moderate, the region offers a variety of routes ranging from moderate to high difficulty, requiring good to very good physical condition. Some more demanding routes can exceed 5 hours and accumulate over 900 meters of elevation gain.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is predominantly mountainous and rolling, featuring numerous climbs and descents. While most surfaces are well-paved, some segments on more challenging routes might include unpaved sections where you may need to push your bike. You'll also find flatter stretches along rivers like the Tormes.

faqs.question: What natural features and landscapes can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: You'll experience a spectacular natural environment with majestic peaks, lush valleys, and crystalline rivers. The route passes through oak and pine forests, alpine meadows, and offers impressive panoramic views, including vistas of the Sierra de Gredos mountains. Keep an eye out for wildflowers and traditional villages.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes by several interesting spots. You'll go through the settlement of Martínez and the Valdejimena Hermitage. Another settlement you'll encounter is Revalbos ups and downs, and you'll also pass by El Mirón Castle.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Villages of the Sierra de Ávila — circular?
faqs.answer: The region is generally best for cycling from spring to autumn. Spring offers vibrant wildflowers, while autumn provides pleasant temperatures and beautiful foliage. Summers can be warm, especially at lower elevations, so consider starting early to avoid the midday heat.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Villages of the Sierra de Ávila — circular route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking facilities for this exact route are not detailed, the region generally offers parking in and around the villages. Look for public parking areas in towns like Martínez or near other starting points in the Sierra de Ávila villages. Many routes utilize quiet secondary roads, making village access straightforward.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily designed for race biking on roads. While dogs are generally welcome in the natural areas of Sierra de Ávila, bringing them along on a race bike tour can be challenging. If you plan to cycle with a dog, ensure they are safely secured in a bike trailer or carrier, and always keep them on a leash when off the bike, especially in villages or near wildlife.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this specific route. It uses public roads and trails. However, if you venture into specific protected areas or private lands, always check local regulations.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with the Ruta de los Lavaderos (7% of the route), Ruta de Carlos V (4%), Ruta de los Esbaruzaderos (3%), and the Cordel de Extremadura a las Montañas de Santander (2%). There are also small segments of the Camino del Molino.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay overnight along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through charming traditional villages where you can find local gastronomy. You can enjoy regional specialties like 'Judías de El Barco' and Iberian ham. Many villages offer cafes, pubs, and accommodation options that are cyclist-friendly, making it easy to refuel or stay overnight.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I see while cycling?
faqs.answer: The rich natural environment provides opportunities to spot various wildlife. You might see birds of prey such as eagles and vultures, as well as larger mammals like deer and ibex, especially in the more remote, mountainous sections.
